system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
# Task C - Health app data privacy policy

## Brief

A direct-to-consumer health app (~2M monthly active users) is launching an AI-powered health coaching feature. The product team must choose a primary data policy that governs how user health data is processed by the AI. The choice has implications for capability, privacy, regulatory exposure, cost, and trust. The CEO asks for a primary recommendation.

## Options (elements E1-E5, anonymized later)

**Option A:** Local-only processing. All AI inference happens on the user's device. No health data leaves the phone. The model is smaller (capability ceiling 60-70% of cloud-equivalent) but privacy is structurally maximal.

**Option B:** Encrypted cloud with no training. User health data sent to the cloud encrypted, decrypted only at inference time in a confidential-computing enclave, never used to train any model. Full capability, ~3x infrastructure cost.

**Option C:** Cloud with opt-out training. Data sent to the cloud, used for personalization by default, used for model improvement unless user opts out. Full capability, standard cost, regulatory grey area in some jurisdictions.

**Option D:** Cloud with opt-in training only. Data sent to the cloud and used for personalization; used for model improvement only with explicit opt-in. Default-on opt-in flow with clear UX. Capability slightly degraded vs C by default (less training data).

**Option E:** Federated learning. Personalization happens locally; aggregate model updates sent to cloud, individual data does not leave the device. Privacy preserved at architectural level. Capability and reliability still under active research; deployment complexity is high.

## Task for each agent

Recommend the most appropriate policy and explain why. 200-400 words. Advocate a position rather than enumerating all options.

## Why this task

- Different domain from A (capital allocation) and B (organizational diagnosis): product ethics with regulatory and technical dimensions.
- Multi-axis tradeoffs: privacy / capability / cost / trust / complexity.
- No clear right answer; reasonable agents will weight axes differently.
- 5 elements suitable for triadic procedure.
- Cross-validates whether method works on a third domain not seen in pilot.


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
raw_response_content:
# Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with No Training

For a direct-to-consumer health app at 2M MAU launching an AI coaching feature, I recommend **Option B: encrypted cloud processing in a confidential-computing enclave with no training on user data.**

The decisive consideration is the asymmetric risk profile of health data. Unlike browsing history or shopping behavior, health data is durably sensitive — a leak or misuse incident can produce regulatory action (HIPAA-adjacent scrutiny in the US, GDPR Article 9 special-category rules in the EU), litigation, and irreversible trust damage. For a consumer health brand, trust is the core asset that compounds retention and word-of-mouth growth. Policies that create even a perception of "your health data trains our AI" are fragile against a single bad news cycle.

Option B is the only choice that combines **full model capability** with a **structurally clean privacy story** that can be explained in one sentence: *"Your data is encrypted, used only to answer your questions, and never used to train our models."* That sentence survives press scrutiny, regulator questions, and competitor attacks.

Why not the alternatives?

- **A (local-only)** sacrifices 30-40% of capability. In a coaching product where quality directly determines health outcomes and competitive differentiation against well-funded cloud-AI rivals, this is too steep.
- **C (opt-out training)** is the worst option: it monetizes the most sensitive data category through dark-pattern defaults and invites regulatory action in the EU, UK, California, and increasingly elsewhere.
- **D (opt-in training)** is defensible but introduces a consent surface that complicates the narrative and creates a two-tier user base. The training-data upside is modest given that high-quality health coaching improvements come more from clinical expertise and curated data than from raw user logs.
- **E (federated learning)** is technically appealing but immature for production health applications. Shipping a flagship feature on research-grade infrastructure is the wrong risk to take at launch.

The ~3x infrastructure cost is real but bounded and falls over time as confidential computing matures (AWS Nitro, Azure Confidential VMs, GCP Confidential Space are all production-ready). At 2M MAU, infrastructure is unlikely to be the binding constraint on the business; trust and regulatory standing are.

**Ship B at launch. Revisit E in 18-24 months if federated learning matures.**
